title: Conservation of Energy
subtitle: Energy cannot be created or destroyed

steps.title: Quick question first…
steps.question: A ball is dropped and bounces back to a lower height than it started. Where has the 'missing' energy gone?
options: The energy was destroyed on impact
The energy was transferred to thermal energy stores of the ball and surroundings
The energy disappeared into the ground
steps.misconceptionFeedback: ⚠️ Common misconception! Energy is NEVER destroyed. The 'missing' energy has been transferred to thermal energy stores of the ball, the floor, and the surrounding air during the impact. This is called energy dissipation.
steps.correctFeedback: ✓ Correct! The principle of conservation of energy says energy is never created or destroyed. The ball doesn’t bounce as high because some energy was transferred to thermal stores during the deformation on impact. This is energy dissipation.
steps.bestSource: BEST resource: AQA GCSE Physics specification 4.1.2 — Conservation and dissipation of energy

steps.title: Conservation of Energy
steps.bodyHTML: The law of conservation of energy states that energy can be transferred between stores, but cannot be created or destroyed. The total energy of a closed system always stays the same. In real systems, energy is often dissipated (spread out) to thermal energy stores of the surroundings — this energy is not 'lost', just less useful.
steps.keyEquation: Total energy before = Total energy after

relationships: GPE at top = KE at bottom (if no air resistance)
GPE at top = KE at bottom + thermal (with air resistance)

commonErrors: Saying energy is 'lost' or 'used up' — say 'dissipated' or 'transferred to thermal stores'
Forgetting to include dissipated energy when balancing energy totals
Confusing conservation of energy with efficiency

steps.title: See it in action
steps.instruction: Watch how GPE converts to KE as the object falls. Drag the friction slider to see how energy is dissipated to thermal stores.

aqa.questionText: A ball of mass 0.5 kg is dropped from a height of 8 m. Calculate its speed just before hitting the ground. Assume no air resistance. (g = 9.8 N/kg)

steps.label: Apply conservation
steps.content: No air resistance, so all GPE converts to KE:

edexcel.questionText: A pendulum bob of mass 0.2 kg is raised 0.4 m above its lowest point. Calculate its speed at the lowest point. (g = 9.8 N/kg)

ocr-gateway.questionText: A stone of mass 0.3 kg is dropped from 5 m. Calculate its kinetic energy just before it hits the ground. (g = 10 N/kg)

steps.label: Apply conservation
steps.content: By conservation: KE at bottom = GPE at top (no air resistance):
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — conservation; 1 mark — correct answer + unit

ocr-21c.questionText: A diver of mass 60 kg jumps from a 10 m board. Calculate her speed when entering the water. Ignore air resistance. (g = 9.8 N/kg)

wjec.questionText: A roller coaster car of mass 500 kg is at the top of a 20 m drop. Calculate its speed at the bottom. Ignore friction. (g = 10 N/kg)

aqa.questionText: A ball of mass 0.4 kg is dropped from 5 m. What is its speed just before hitting the ground? Ignore air resistance. (g = 10 N/kg)
aqa.markSchemeAward: 1 mark GPE · 1 mark conservation · 1 mark correct speed + unit
aqa.commonMistake: Option A comes from forgetting to use √(2Ek/m) and just dividing energy by mass.

edexcel.questionText: A 2 kg object falls from 3.2 m. What is its KE just before hitting the ground? (g = 10 N/kg, ignore air resistance)
edexcel.markSchemeAward: 1 mark conservation · 1 mark correct answer + unit

ocr-gateway.questionText: A skier of mass 70 kg starts from rest at the top of a 15 m slope. What is the maximum KE at the bottom? (g = 10 N/kg)
ocr-gateway.markSchemeAward: 1 mark GPE calculation · 1 mark conservation applied

ocr-21c.questionText: A 1.5 kg coconut falls from 8 m. What is its speed just before hitting the ground? (g = 10 N/kg)
ocr-21c.markSchemeAward: 1 mark method · 1 mark correct answer + unit

wjec.questionText: A 0.8 kg ball is dropped from 10 m. What is the KE just before hitting the ground? (g = 10 N/kg)

equation.canonical: Total energy before = Total energy after
equation.examTip: When a question says 'ignore air resistance' or 'no friction', it means all GPE converts to KE. If it says 'in practice', some energy is dissipated to thermal stores.

boardNotes.aqa: Not a formula — a principle. Must explain using correct energy store language.
boardNotes.edexcel: Must be able to calculate using GPE = KE. Show all working.
boardNotes.ocr-gateway: Commonly tested in multi-step calculations combining Ep = mgh and Ek = ½mv².
boardNotes.ocr-21c: May ask for descriptions of energy transfers in everyday scenarios.
